title: The Slow Road, with You Walking Calmly Down It
subtitle: A five-minute visualisation for the urge to fix the body fast
excerpt: A five-minute visualisation for when the urge to change the body fast is loud. The image is a long road with you walking it calmly. Used to settle the rush-and-fix energy before the next meal, the next workout, the next decision.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The long-road image is used across visualisation work for any long-arc goal that cannot be rushed.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the urge to change the body fast is loud and the next meal, the next workout, or the next decision is about to be made from that urge.

content.text: A five-minute visualisation for the rush-and-fix energy that arrives when the body feels wrong. The image is a long road with you walking it. The walk is calm, the road is long, and the destination does not move.
New to visualisation as a practice? Read

A long road. Country road, not motorway. There is a low stone wall on one side and grass on the other. The road dips and rises a little. The light is the soft light of late afternoon. The road goes on as far as you can see.
Sit somewhere comfortable. The eyes can close or stay soft. Take three slow breaths.
Picture yourself at the start of the road. You can see what you are wearing. The shoes are comfortable. Your hands are at your sides. You take the first step.

You walk slowly. Not slow on purpose. Slow because there is nowhere to be by a deadline. You can hear your own steps. You can hear the small sounds of the day around you: a bird, a tractor in the distance, the wind in the grass.

The road keeps going. You keep walking. You are not in a hurry. You are not slowing down to wait either. You are walking at the pace your body would walk if no one were watching it and there were no clock.
If the mind tries to push the picture faster, gently bring it back to the walk. Same pace. Same road. Same calm.
When the five minutes is up, you do not have to reach a destination. The road is still long. You stop walking and stand still on the road for one breath. Then open your eyes.
Carry the pace of the walk into the next thing you do. The body is on the road. The road is long. You can walk it at this pace.
Original to homemade.education. The long-road image is used across visualisation work for any goal that cannot be rushed (a slow body change, a slow business build, a slow recovery).
